unscored_issues.title: Several misspellings in doctl
unscored_issues.body:
### Overview

There are several misspellings in our codebase. These can be found with the Go tool [misspell](https://github.com/client9/misspell).

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you're using the `misspell` tool, you'll notice that there are quite a few issues reported. Most of these are in vendored code, so to make it easier, these are the only ones affecting this actual codebase:
    1. `commands/account.go:27:65`: "retreive" is a misspelling of "retrieve"
    2. `commands/certificates.go:85:36`: "comfirmation" is a misspelling of "confirmation"
    3. `commands/image_actions.go:48:123`: "inlcuding" is a misspelling of "including"
    4. `commands/completion.go:165:35`: "commmand" is a misspelling of "command"
    5. `integration/cdn_create_test.go:84:11`: "orgin" is a misspelling of "origin"
    6. `integration/cdn_create_test.go:100:46`: "orgin" is a misspelling of "origin"
3. Once those misspellings have been fixed, open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #870" which will close this issue upon being merged.

unscored_issues.title: Top-level doctl package fails golint check
Apps integration test fails to check a returned error
unscored_issues.body:
### Overview

Our [Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl) shows that we're failing to get a perfect score on the `ineffassign` check due to a [single error](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl#ineffassign). That error is in our integration testing suite, and it's that our apps integration test fails to check a returned error.

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you haven't already, install Go through their [official installer](https://golang.org/dl/).
3. In [`doctl/integration/apps_spec_test.go:145`](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/master/integration/apps_spec_test.go#L145), we're failing to check the `err` returned from the `json.Marshal(testAppSpec)` call. Just below that line, you'll need to add an `expect.NoError(err)` statement.
4. Open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #867" which will close this issue upon being merged.

unscored_issues.title: Run gofmt on doctl's codebase
unscored_issues.body:
### Overview

Right now, [Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl) reports that we have so gofmt failures in our codebase.

Typically, these can fixed by running `gofmt -s -w ./` from the root of the project.

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you haven't already, install Go through their [official installer](https://golang.org/dl/).
3. From the root of this project, run `gofmt -s -w ./`. The `-s` flag is short for "simplify", and will make some changes to make the code simpler. The `-w` flag is short for "write", which means that the changes will be written to disk instead of shown in stdout.
4. Open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #866" which will close this issue upon being merged.

````
unscored_issues.title: Support configuring new auth context without interactivity
unscored_issues.body:
In some cases, it can be useful to configure `doctl` non-interactively. Currently, we support this when setting up the default context. For example, running the following will validate the token and add it to the `doctl` config file without additional user interaction:

```
doctl auth init -t $DO_TOKEN
Using token [asupersekrettokenthatisverylongidontwanttotypeit]

Validating token... OK
```

If the `--context` flag is added to that command, the token is ignored and the user is prompted to enter the token manually:

```
doctl auth init --context new-context -t $DO_TOKEN
DigitalOcean access token: 
```

The behaviour should be consistent. When passing a token and a context together to `doctl auth init`, we should validate the token and configure a new context to use it without additional user input.

unscored_issues.title: Support resource names instead of IDs in more places
unscored_issues.body:
Hi,

As I am trying to create my own DigitalOcean provisioning automation using doctl, I find myself having to create more complicated scripts due to the fact that some (most?) of the doctl commands - when they refer to a specific resource - expect ID instead of the friendly name I have given it.

Some commands are friendly and know how to handle names, like this one:

```shell
# doctl compute droplet tag <droplet-id|droplet-name> 
$ doctl compute droplet tag sandbox --tag-name development
```

While other commands work only with ID, like this one:

```shell
# doctl compute firewall add-droplets <id> --droplet-ids <id,id,...>
$ doctl compute firewall add-droplets d9...28 --droplet-ids 1234
```

and it would make life so much easier if we could use names here, like:

```shell
$ doctl compute firewall add-droplets my-default-firewall --droplets sandbox,development
```

Any chance this will be supported?

unscored_issues.title: Context switching issues
unscored_issues.body:
I am not sure if I am doing it wrong, but the auth switching feature does not work as expected for me.

### 1. Possible bug: `DIGITALOCEAN_CONTEXT` not working

Following [these instructions][1], I understand that setting the `DIGITALOCEAN_CONTEXT` environment variable is the equivalent of using the `--context <context>` flag.

I expected this to work:

```
$ DIGITALOCEAN_CONTEXT=dannyb doctl account get
```

It does not. The account selected is **not** the one specified in the context.

If this environment variable is only intended to work with the `doctl auth init` command, than

1. it did not work there as well, and
2. it should work across commands. This will allow using some `.envrc` file that can be present on a per-directory basis, and set different contexts without having to type `--context <context>` all the time.

### 2. `doctl auth switch` confusion

Following the pattern of all other commands in the `doctl` CLI, I intuitively typed:

```
$ doctl auth context switch new-context
```

Of course, this does not work (but did not raise an error), as it expects `--context <context>` since it is a global flag.
Since this command is intended to do context switching, I would consider one of these options:

1. Allow this syntax to work: `doctl auth context switch new-context` (this would be my preference. The --context flag should NOT be accepted in this command).
2. Make the `--context` argument mandatory when running this command.
3. Raise an error when running without it, or when running with the extra (ignored) positional parameter, so we know we did something wrong.


[1]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl#logging-in-to-multiple-digitalocean-accounts

unscored_issues.title: Provide instructions with permission denied error when using Snap without kubeconfig access
unscored_issues.body:
**Problem**

Similar to #475. Looks like [we will not be given](https://forum.snapcraft.io/t/personal-files-request-for-doctl-was-classic-confinement-request-doctl/11785/12) auto-connect for write access to kubeconfig.

When installed as an Ubuntu snap, doctl needs to be explicitly given permission to access `~/.kube/config` for use with the `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save` sub-command.

Attempting to save a kubeconfig file before granting those permissions results in an error:

    Error: open /home/asb/.kube/config.lock: permission denied

Unless you have read the README, this error message can be confusing and does not provide a path forward for the user.

**Proposed Solution**

When `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save` is run in a snap environment (hint: https://docs.snapcraft.io/environment-variables) without first being granted access to the doctl:kube-config plug, we should catch the permissions error (or check first if possible) and present the user with an error message with a clear path to resolving the problem. For example:

    Error: open /home/asb/.kube/config.lock: permission denied

    Using the doctl Snap? Grant access to the doctl:kube-config plug to use this command.

        sudo snap connect doctl:kube-config

unscored_issues.title: Provide instructions with permission denied error when using Snap without SSH key access
unscored_issues.body:
**Problem**

When installed as an Ubuntu snap, doctl needs to be explicitly given permission to access SSH keys for use with the `doctl compute ssh` sub-command. For details, see:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl#using-doctl-compute-ssh

Attempting to SSH to a Droplet before granting those permissions results in an error:

    Error: fork/exec /usr/bin/ssh: permission denied

Unless you have read the README, this error message can be confusing and does not provide a path forward for the user.

**Proposed Solution**

When `doctl compute ssh` is run in a snap environment (hint: https://docs.snapcraft.io/environment-variables) without first being granted access to the ssh-keys interface, we should catch the permission denied error (or check first if possible) and present the user with an error message with a clear path to resolving the problem. For example:

    Error: fork/exec /usr/bin/ssh: permission denied

    Using the doctl Snap? Grant access to the ssh-keys interface to use this command.

        sudo snap connect doctl:ssh-keys :ssh-keys

unscored_issues.title: Determine config file's default location at runtime
unscored_issues.body:
Currently `doctl`'s help output specifies that the default location of the config file is `$HOME/.config/doctl/config.yaml`

```
  -c, --config string         config file (default is $HOME/.config/doctl/config.yaml)
```

This is hardcoded at:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/e5406406016386e95931501fc00d082691189f65/commands/doit.go#L82

In certain environments, this is not true. For instance, on Linux the location of the config file is determined by the `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` environment variable. On Windows, `LOCALAPPDATA` is used. Additionally when using the Ubuntu snap, `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is overridden leading to misleading help output. E.g.

```
$ locate doctl | grep '\.config/doctl/config.yaml'
/home/asb/.config/doctl/config.yaml
/home/asb/snap/doctl/19/.config/doctl/config.yaml
```

Instead of hardcoding this path in the help output, we should determine config file's default location at runtime.

Hint: A helper function already exists for this:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/e5406406016386e95931501fc00d082691189f65/commands/doit.go#L158-L160

unscored_issues.title: Kubernetes cluster deletion leaves dangling loadbalancer(s)
unscored_issues.body:
## What happens
The following sequence of steps should reproduce the problem:

```
doctl kubernetes cluster create meow
```
Wait for cluster provisioning to finish, then:

```
kubectl --context=do-nyc1-meow apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/digitalocean/digitalocean-cloud-controller-manager/master/docs/controllers/services/examples/http-nginx.yml
```

Wait for the load balancer to appear in the DO control panel (or in the output of `doctl compute load-balancer list`).

```
doctl kubernetes cluster delete meow
```

The cluster should become unavailable almost immediately, but you should continue to see the load balancer in the DO control panel (or in the output of `doctl compute load-balancer list`) until you explicitly do something to delete it.

## What I expect to happen
From my point of view as the user it seems reasonable to expect the loadbalancer to be garbage collected when tearing down the cluster since it was created as a result of an interaction with the cluster.

unscored_issues.title: fetching of Kubernetes credentials / configuration fails occasionally with a "500 Server Error"
unscored_issues.body:
When you create a Kubernetes cluster, doctl fetches the configuration and integrates it into the user kubeconfig file. I noticed that this operation is failing occasionally with a HTTP 500 error. Example:

```
$ doctl kubernetes cluster create test-cluster --count 1 --region fra1 --version 1.13.1-do.2
Notice: cluster created, fetching credentials

Warning: couldn't get credentials for cluster, it will not be added to your kubeconfig: GET https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/kubernetes/clusters/059873d7-a92c-447b-99f7-xxxxxxxxxxxx/kubeconfig: 500 Server Error
```

If I were to put this into numbers, I'd say that the failure happens to me at 10 to 15 percent of my cluster creation invocations.

FWIW, I tend to use the FRA1 region.

unscored_issues.title: The renew command in warning is missing cluster
unscored_issues.body:
When I run `doctl k8s cluster create` command the command I see the following warning which is useful but the command seems to be wrong.

```
Warning: cluster credentials will expire on <DATETIME>, renew with: "doctl k8s kubeconfig save <NAME>"
```

It should be `"doctl k8s **cluster** kubeconfig save <NAME>"`

unscored_issues.title: Node sized differs in doctl and webUI
Exclude access token from config file if defined in environment variable
unscored_issues.body:
I like to keep my config files in a private GitHub repo so that I can track the changes I make to them. However, `doctl` makes this difficult because the access token is included in `~/.config/doctl/config.yaml`.

I deleted the file and instead use an environment variable to define the access token. This worked, but now I don't have a config file. Running `doctl auth init` again puts the config file back, but with the access token in it.

Maybe it's possible to exclude the access token during `doctl auth init` if it's already defined by an environment variable?

unscored_issues.title: --ip-address should be optional in doctl compute domain create
unscored_issues.body:
The flag `--ip-address` is required in `doctl compute domain create`:

```
create domain

Usage:
  doctl compute domain create <domain> [flags]

Aliases:
  create, c


Flags:
      --format string       Columns for output in a comma separated list. Possible values: Domain,TTL
      --ip-address string   IP address (required)
      --no-header           hide headers
```

But `ip_address` is optional in the API:

https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/v2/#create-a-new-domain

What about making the flag optional in `doctl` too?

unscored_issues.title: Add "detach" and "attach" functionality for Volumes

unscored_issues.title: Update snap & metadata
unscored_issues.body:
Hi. It seems the snap of doctl is a little outdated. Can it be updated to 1.8.3?

```
channels:                   
  stable:    1.8.2 (15) 3MB -
  candidate: 1.6.0 (2)  3MB -
  beta:      1.6.0 (2)  3MB -
  edge:      1.7.0 (13) 3MB -
alan@hal:~$ 
```

I'd recommend closing the non-stable channels while you're at it if you're not using them. `snapcraft close doctl candidate beta edge`

While we're at it, the metadata for doctl says the owner of the snap is "Engineering".

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1841272/43640398-b867b08c-9717-11e8-9d8f-d0130217f960.png)

I'd recommend changing that to "DigitalOcean" :) Unfortunately we have a store bug which means the process is a little convoluted:-

1. Go to https://login.ubuntu.com/
2. Change "Your Name" field
3. Save
4. Go to https://dashboard.snapcraft.io/
5. Logout
6. Login
7. Go to https://dashboard.snapcraft.io/dev/account/
8. Check Full Name is changed

Once done I think we should add a "Verified" publisher tag (which I will do) so users can be confident the snap comes from the upstream developers.

unscored_issues.title: Build-from-source misreports version
unscored_issues.body:
If you build from source, you get misleading version output as the version reported is hardcoded in `doit.go`:
```
        // DoitVersion is doit's version.
        DoitVersion = Version{
                Major: 1,
                Minor: 7,
                Patch: 2,
                Label: "stable",
        }
````

Suggested fix: Derive the version to report from `git describe`.

unscored_issues.title: Suggestion: Show public images by default
unscored_issues.body:
Hi, I think the required `--public` flag for `doctl image list` is a bit counterintuitive. It seems to have tripped up others in the past and this was changed for `list-distribution` and `list-application` in #280. 

I think it is a better UX for a list command to list all resources and then you should opt in to filtering them if you want. This would also match the behavior of `list-distribution` and `list-application`. Moreover, newcomers to the CLI are probably using `image list` to find an image slug to use in other commands, so making `--public=true` the default will help this beginner use case.

unscored_issues.title: Dockerfile does not support SSH
unscored_issues.body:
The Dockerfile of this Repository does not allow the usage of the SSH command!

Openssh is missing.

here is a working example:

```
FROM alpine:3.5

ENV DOCTL_VERSION=1.8.0

RUN apk add --no-cache curl openssh

RUN mkdir /root/.ssh/

ARG HOST_IP
RUN ssh-keyscan -H $HOST_IP >> /root/.ssh/known_hosts

ARG SSH_KEY
RUN echo "$SSH_KEY" > /root/.ssh/id_rsa
RUN chmod 600 /root/.ssh/id_rsa

ARG SSH_KEY_PUB
RUN echo "$SSH_KEY_PUB" > /root/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
RUN chmod 600 /root/.ssh/id_rsa.pub

RUN mkdir /lib64 && ln -s /lib/libc.musl-x86_64.so.1 /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2

WORKDIR /app

RUN curl -L https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/download/v${DOCTL_VERSION}/doctl-${DOCTL_VERSION}-linux-amd64.tar.gz  | tar xz

ENTRYPOINT ["./doctl"]
```
Src: https://github.com/demokratie-live/democracy-development/blob/master/doctl

And here is how you buidl and call it (./doctl referes to the dockerfile):

```
docker build -t doctl --build-arg SSH_KEY="$(cat ./ssh_key)" --build-arg SSH_KEY_PUB="$(cat ./ssh_key.pub)" --build-arg HOST_IP="${DIGITAL_OCEAN_ALPHA_HOST}" - < ./doctl
```

```
docker run --rm -e DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN="${DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN}" doctl compute ssh ${DIGITALOCEAN_ALPHA_SERVER_DROPLET_NAME} --ssh-user ${DIGITALOCEAN_ALPHA_SERVER_USER} --ssh-command "cd ~/democracy-development && ./deploy-production.sh $TRAVIS_TAG"
```

src: https://github.com/demokratie-live/democracy-development/blob/master/.travis.yml#L78

**Please correct your docker file to at least include the openssh package - even better would be to provide something like the above as seperate file including instructions**

```sh
/usr/local/bin/zsh
```

When I run `doctl auth init -t mytoken` I expect to not be prompted for an access token.
Using the long form `--access-token` instead of `-t` makes no difference.

```sh
$ doctl auth init -t $DO_ACCESS_TOKEN
DigitalOcean access token:    # You can type whatever here. Won't matter.
```

If `$DO_ACCESS_TOKEN` is valid, `doctl auth init` succeeds.

unscored_issues.title: Feature Request: Multiple Accounts
unscored_issues.body:
I am not sure what it would look like for the command syntax, but multiple accounts would be an excellent feature.

Personally, I set up all my clients on their own DO account, for billing purposes, so doctl is borderline unusable for quick tasks.

I see two simple options for the syntax and implmenetation (there may be more):

1) add another auth command with shortcuts - `doctl auth switch [accountnickname]` switches the context to the other account. This is backward compatible. 

2) The breaking change option would be specifying the account nickname in every command. Also more stateless though.

unscored_issues.title: Feature Request: droplet-action by hostname, when possible
unscored_issues.body:
currently, if I need to bounce my box, I need two commands to reboot 

```
# Find the int ID of the droplet
doctl compute droplet ls | grep "example.com"

# Use the ID above to run command on example.com (id 123456)
doctl compute droplet-action power-cycle 123456
```
This requires one extra step than one would typically expect.

However, most users, I would imagine, only have a few boxes. I'd like to propose another syntax combining these two commands into a single shorthand (commands by hostname):

```
doctl compute droplet-action power-cycle example.com
```
which would be functionally equivilent to the bash command (yes, I know the grep isn't precise, just a rough example):
```
doctl compute droplet-action power-cycle  `doctl compute d ls | grep "example.com" | awk '{print $1}'`
```

When the number of droplets with the hostname of `example.com` = 1, this should introduce no logical issues.

Thoughts?

unscored_issues.title: Transfer ownership of doctl snap to you
unscored_issues.body:
[Previously](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/issues/136), snap support was discussed, and the [yaml](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/master/snap/snapcraft.yaml) is now upstream. We have a snap in the store, which is maintained by our community [snapcrafters](https://github.com/snapcrafters/doctl). 

However we'd prefer that, now you have a yaml in your master branch, we transition the ownership of the snap upstream to you. This should be a fairly painless process, which we can help navigate. Essentially, we need to have:-

  * You register with the snap store at http://dashboard.snapcraft.io/
  * Let us know your account details
  * We request the snap is moved across to your account
  * You hook up our [build service](http://build.snapcraft.io) to push automatically to the store - or you use whatever CI system you prefer to push to the store.

Once you've got an account registered in the store, we can move forward. Do let us know if you have any problems.

unscored_issues.title: proposal: make doctl part of chocolatey package manager
unscored_issues.body:
For some time now, `doctl` is part of Homebrew package manager for macOS, and from not so long ago, Snap for Linux joined the party. That means that we don't support only Windows package managers.

As far as I know, Windows still don't have their official solution, but there are some community-driven ones. [Chocolatey](https://chocolatey.org/) seems like one of those, and most popular one, looking by community reviews.
Looking by their [catalog](https://chocolatey.org/packages), there are many popular packages and applications going from casual ones like WinRAR, VLC to developer oriented ones like Node.js, Docker, Kubernetes...

I don't use Windows for long time now, so I'm not really in their community and I don't know about state of package management for it. Searching the Internet and looking by some tweets, it seems like above solution is good to go for Windows.
If you know better and more popular solution, please comment below.

I would like to know, are you interested in adding `doctl` there. If yes, I could see could I be helpful with this and bring needed scripts and so on. I think I can get handy copy of Windows so I guess I can build some testing env.

Also, maybe somebody from DO legal team should check their license requirements. As far as I understand, it's free for Open Source projects to everybody, including companies, but it doesn't hurt to be checked by somebody who knows about it.

Fire up your opinions and critics. :+1: 

/cc @mauricio @viola @caueguerra @lxfontes @hugocorbucci @magicgrl111

unscored_issues.title: accept name of ssh key when specifying --ssh-keys from cli
unscored_issues.body:
Having to find the fingerprint and provide that to `doctl` is a bit of a pain. It would be a much better user experience if I were able to use the name of my key in the digitalocean interface instead of the fingerprint of the public key in order to identify it. 

i.e this is a bit of a pain:

```
# ssh-keygen -E md5 -lf /path/to/key.pub
2048 MD5:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx foo@gmail.com (RSA)
# doctl compute droplet create --image fedora-25-x64-atomic --region nyc3 --size 16gb --ssh-keys xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx  atomicdroplet
```

this would be better:

```
# doctl compute droplet create --image fedora-25-x64-atomic --region nyc3 --size 16gb --ssh-keys name-of-key  atomicdroplet
```

unscored_issues.title: Show features enabled in droplet list table
unscored_issues.body: I was experimenting with monitoring on DigitalOcean and released that doctl doesn't explicitly show what features are enabled on the droplet. I think it would be nice if when listing the droplets, it shows which droplets have which features enabled. Willing to send a PR for this if it makes sense to do so.

unscored_issues.title: doctl should error when enabling monitoring on unsupported images
unscored_issues.body:
I understand that the monitoring feature that has been [recently announced][1] is not supported on base images such as Fedora.
However, if I `doctl compute d c XX --enable-monitoring --ssh-keys keyid ....`, it does create the droplet and echos no errors or warnings. But when I try to ssh into it afterwards the connection is refused.  
I guess that the droplet initialization process is failing each time because of the `--enable-monitoring` flag, thus keeping me out until I manually reset root password from the web console -which I don't always have access to.

If I my guess is right, I think _doctl_ should echo a warning when `--enabled-monitoring` is passed alongside an unsupported base image.

[1]: https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/an-introduction-to-digitalocean-monitoring

unscored_issues.title: --volumes requires a UUID while all other options can handle names/slugs
unscored_issues.body:
I recently tried building a droplet using the following cli:

```
doctl compute droplet create meenirx --enable-ipv6 \
  --enable-private-networking --image freebsd-11-0-x64-zfs \
  --size 512mb --ssh-keys 1234567 --volumes volume-fra1-01 \
  --region fra1
```

and got the following response from the API:

```
Error: POST https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/droplets: 500 (request "9b12fd54-ba34-4106-8244-8f0f2ca7c599")
  Looks like something went wrong. If this continues, please open a support ticket so we can help you out.
```

All other options are happy to take a name / slug, only volumes requires a UUID. That seems like inconsistent UI. If there's no way around this, the least we could do would be to add a check on doctl's side before sending it off to the API

Following Droplets will be deleted:
ID		Name			Public IPv4	Private IPv4	Public IPv6	Memory	VCPUs	Disk	Region	Image			Status	Tags

unscored_issues.title: pass ssh command line down to the actual ssh
unscored_issues.body:

unscored_issues.title: Why is there difference between 'tag-name' and 'tag-names' flags?
unscored_issues.body:
Just a friendly question - but by was `tag-name` left to error if tag is non-existing? :)
As far as I remember, earlier it would just create Droplet but will not apply tag if it doesn't exist.

Now there is API update and new command in doctl `tag-names` which supports this by default.
Why we have two commands that basically do the same?
Following two commands have same result if `tag1` exists 
```
doctl compute droplet create doctl-2 --image 20822342 --region nyc1 --size 20gb --tag-name tag1
doctl compute droplet create doctl-2 --image 20822342 --region nyc1 --size 20gb --tag-names tag1
```
Yes first is taking string and second can take array but if only one is specified it'll do the same work.

So I think it could be that create new tag is default behavior and make one command from this two. I wanted to know why this isn't done (why we have 2 commands now)? :) I guess I don't see something obvious but I think it's OK. :+1: 

cc @hugocorbucci @daveworth @mchitten

unscored_issues.title: [Idea] Add SCP command do doctl
unscored_issues.body:
I'm thinking about idea of implementing `scp` into `doctl`, so you can easy upload or download files from your droplet.
It will help DigitalOcean users to have one tool, which does everything, from managing droplets, images... to SSH access and possibly SCP. Windows users will benefit too, as they don't have integrated SCP in shell.

I got idea of making it in two commands:
`doctl compute download <droplet-id> <remote-location> <local-location>`
`doctl compute upload <droplet-id> <local-location> <remote-location>`

As far as I researched, I don't think it would be hard to make it.
For Linux/OS X users, we can use system SCP (like now SSH, external). It should work well.
Yet for Windows users I found two SCP packages:
[Pacakge `scp` from github.com/tmc/scp](https://godoc.org/github.com/tmc/scp).
[Package `goScp` from github.com/kkirsche/go-scp](https://godoc.org/github.com/kkirsche/go-scp).

I wanted to know does doctl collaborators/DigitalOcean thought about this idea. Would it be useful, is it worth to add it, is it good idea?
Maybe, I could do it myself, but I want to hear comments on it :)

unscored_issues.title: doctl integration with snap package manager (not only for Ubuntu[1])
unscored_issues.body:
It is possible to package doctl using snap. Advantages:
- you can advertise the rigorous application isolation;
- it will update automatically and transactionally;
- for example, after an update, if the new release is broken the user can easily roolback to the last working version.
- not only for x64 (for example, I have armhf and arm64 packages published).

So doctl will be always fresh and never broken.

---

In fact, using exactly the binary inside https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/download/v1.4.0/doctl-1.4.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz, I created a snap and installed it locally. That said, I would like to say:
- you, digitalocean can, in minutes create an official account in Ubuntu Store and submit yourself the snap package. I can make a PR.
- I can register a name like doctl-snap and publish the snap myself.

Anyway, even if I submit the package myself, I really appreciate if anyone more confortable with doctl help me assuring package works as expected.

---

[1] snaps should now work, unmodified, on Arch, CentOS, Debian, Elementary, Fedora, Gentoo, RHEL, SUSE... and of course the whole family of *buntu's.

unscored_issues.title: Specifying a nonexisting tag to 'droplet create' gives an error but still creates the droplet
unscored_issues.body:
```
doctl compute droplet create --size 512mb --tag-name test-metadata-issue --image centos-7-2-x64 --region fra1 doctl-issue-test.example.org
```

returns

```
doctl computID      Name                Public IPv4 Public IPv6 Memory  VCPUs   Disk    Region  Image       Status  Tags
22002227    doctl-issue-test.example.org                    512 1   20  fra1    CentOS 7.2 x64  new
create droplet

Usage:
  doctl compute droplet create NAME [NAME ...] [flags]
..snip..

Error: POST https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/tags/test-metadata-issue/resources: 404 The resource you were accessing could not be found.
```

Although you can see some information about a droplet being created at the top, the whole usage information is a long enough wall of text for me to not notice that, but to do notice the error at the end. So I was assuming the droplets weren't created, but it turns out that they actually were.

This is caused by doctl first creating the machine, and then running the tag API. As these are [not atomic](https://digitalocean.uservoice.com/forums/136585-digitalocean/suggestions/15593823-allow-setting-tags-when-creating-a-droplet), using a nonexisting tag will not prevent you from creating the droplet. But it's not really obvious that this has happened.

Unless the API is improved, the best fix for the client would probably to verify the tag exists before using them.

unscored_issues.title: SSH Client doesn't work on Windows
unscored_issues.body:
It happens on current master (a57555c), when trying to connect to a droplet from Windows 10 in VirtualBox.

There are 2 different problems:

**1/ When trying to connect with a SSH key that isn't password protected, the error is**:

```
doctl.exe compute ssh playground --ssh-key-path D:\ssh-tmp-keys\id_rsa_without_password
Error: read /dev/stdout: The handle is invalid.
```

The problem is that [this line](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/a57555c195d06bc7aa5037af77fde0665ad1231f/pkg/ssh/ssh_internal.go#L48) is using `os.Stdout` while it should be using `os.Stdin`.

There's also a logical error [here](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/a57555c195d06bc7aa5037af77fde0665ad1231f/pkg/ssh/ssh.go#L83), we check for the _function scoped_ `err` before starting the shell,  but it can be _non-nil_ because of [this line](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/a57555c195d06bc7aa5037af77fde0665ad1231f/pkg/ssh/ssh.go#L67).

**2/ When trying to connect with a SSH key that is password protected, the error is**:

```
doctl.exe compute ssh playground --ssh-key-path D:\ssh-tmp-keys\id_rsa_with_password
Error: asn1: structure error: tags don't match (16 vs {class:1 tag:6 length:50 isCompound:false}) {optional:false explicit:false application:false defaultValue:<nil> tag:<nil> stringType:0 timeType:0 set:false omitEmpty:false} pkcs1PrivateKey @2
```

The problem is that `ssh.ParsePrivateKey(key)` on [this line](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/a57555c195d06bc7aa5037af77fde0665ad1231f/pkg/ssh/ssh_internal.go#L33) expects a [PEM encoded private key](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/crypto/ssh#ParsePrivateKey) so we have to decrypt it first.

The SSH keys used to reproduce this issue have been generated using:
`ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "t@example.com"`

One key has a passphrase, the other doesn't. They are attached here:
[ssh-test-keys.tar.gz](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/files/380347/ssh-test-keys.tar.gz)

unscored_issues.title: Allow jsonpath traversals through doctl output
unscored_issues.body:
Add a jsonpath option to allow easy retrieval of specific data from a doctl response. Example:

`doctl compute get 1234 --jsonpath "Networks.V4"` would return the IPv4 networks block from the JSON response. JSON output is assumed with the `jsonpath` option.

unscored_issues.title: Add template support to customize outputs
unscored_issues.body: Using Go templates, expose the raw response to the end user, so outputs can be customized. As an example, if you retrieve a droplet with `doctl compute droplet get 1234`, you could retrieve the droplet creation time with `doctl compute droplet get 1234 --template ".CreatedAt".

unscored_issues.title: Replace `doctl auth login` with `doctl auth init`
unscored_issues.body: The `doctl auth login` process has a few issues that doesn't allow it to work the same way on every platform. I'm proposing we replace this with a `doctl auth init` that will take a DO token on the command line and do the initial configuration which will include creating a doctl config file.

unscored_issues.title: git-style aliases
unscored_issues.body:
## What

Allow git-style aliases for commands.
## Why

Some commands are more frequently used than others by users, so allowing people to alias them to their convenience will provide a more ergonomic experience.
## How

Possible impl:
- Add the aliases definitions to the config file.
- Before starting the `cobra` stuff, expand the `os.Args` according to the aliases.

Anyway when attempting to login, it seems that the client is ignoring all the argument flags and not actually running the auth flow (or at least clearly stating what it does) 
![image 2016-03-30 at 4 48 01 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1396368/14148564/ce7fc1f2-f697-11e5-99a7-2309ef03f101.png)

Goob job on a 1.0 attempt though guys, looking forward it working nicer :P

unscored_issues.title: droplet delete name disambiguation
unscored_issues.body:
If you have multiple droplets with the same name, e.g. "ubuntu-2gb-nyc3-01", and issue a droplet delete command, doctl deletes the oldest one.

```
doctl compute droplet delete ubuntu-2gb-nyc3-01
```

This should be documented, or there should be a disambiguation or confirmation prompt.

unscored_issues.title: doctl should support a config file
unscored_issues.body: Currently the doctl command line tool supports retrieving auth from env vars, but I would prefer to store these in a dot file in my home directory.
